1: Psalm 145 carries the unique superscription Tĕhillāh lĕdāwid — "A Praise of David." Tĕhillāh (Praise, hymn of praise) is the singular form of Tĕhillîm — the Hebrew name for the entire book of Psalms. Ps 145 is thus in some sense the book's defining psalm, the one that names the whole collection. "I will exalt you, my God and King (ʾĕlōhay hammelek)" — the title "King" for YHWH is the distinctive claim of the enthronement psalms and of Ps 145 in particular, which develops the divine kingdom (Psalms 145:11–13) as the psalm's central theological theme. The promise "I will bless your name forever (lĕʿôlām waʿed)" frames the entire acrostic: from aleph to taw, for eternity.
2: "Every day I will bless you and praise your name forever and ever" — the temporal extension of praise: "every day (bĕkol-yôm)" and "forever (lĕʿôlām waʿed)" together span the full scope of time. The rabbinic tradition developed from Ps 145 the practice of reciting it three times daily — morning, afternoon, and evening — as a fundamental act of Jewish piety. The Babylonian Talmud (Berakhot 4b) states: "Whoever recites Tĕhillāh lĕdāwid three times each day is assured of a place in the world to come." The psalm's structure (acrostic covering all 22 Hebrew letters) supports its reputation as an all-encompassing praise.
3: "The LORD is great and highly to be praised; his greatness no one can search out" — wĕligdūlātô ʾên ḥēqer (and his greatness has no searching out). The unsearchable divine greatness is an ANE theological commonplace (cf. the Mesopotamian hymns to Marduk and Shamash), but in the Psalter it becomes specifically the greatness of covenant acts — the gĕdullāh (greatness/greatness-in-deed) that Israel has witnessed in the exodus and settlement. "No searching out (ʾên ḥēqer)" — the same phrase used of the divine understanding in Isaiah 40:28 ("his understanding no one can fathom") and in Job 5:9 (the unsearchable wonders of God).
4: "One generation will commend your works to the next and declare your mighty deeds" — dôr lĕdôr yĕšabbaḥ maʿăśêkā (generation to generation will praise your works). The intergenerational transmission of covenant memory is a fundamental Psalter and Deuteronomic concern: Psalms 78:1–8 ("we will tell the next generation the praiseworthy deeds of the LORD"); Deuteronomy 6:20–25 (children ask about the statutes and are told the Exodus story); Psalms 102:18 ("let this be written for a future generation"). The "mighty deeds (gĕbûrōt)" are the acts of divine power — especially the Exodus and covenant-history events — that are the content of this generational transmission.
5: "I will speak of the splendor of your glorious majesty and of your wonderful deeds" — hădār kĕbôd hôdekā (the splendor of the glory of your majesty) — three near-synonyms for divine majesty stacked together. Hādār (splendor, beauty, honor), kābôd (glory, weight, honor), and hôd (majesty, splendor) together create a superlative of superlatives — the most intense possible expression of divine glory. In the ANE royal court, the king's splendor was displayed in his clothing, his throne, his processions, and his palace; the psalm applies all of this vocabulary to YHWH, and then intensifies it beyond any earthly analogy.
6: "People will speak of the power of your awe-inspiring acts, and I will tell of your greatness" — the two subjects of praise in Psalms 145:6 — "people (wĕʿûzuz — the force/power of your terrible acts)" and "I (wĕʾăsappĕrennāh)" — create a movement between communal and individual witness. The "awe-inspiring acts (nôrĕʾōtêkā)" are the terrifying interventions of divine power in history — the plagues, the crossing of the sea, the conquest — that produced both fear in the nations and worship in Israel.
7: "They will overflow with praise for your great goodness and sing aloud of your righteousness" — zēker rab-ṭûbĕkā yaббîʿû (they will overflow/pour out the memory of your great goodness). The verb nābaʿ (to bubble up, overflow, gush) suggests an uncontainable abundance — the praise that cannot be contained. "Great goodness (rab-ṭûb)" — ṭôb (good, goodness, prosperity) in its fullest sense encompasses both moral goodness and the material abundance that flows from YHWH's generosity. "Your righteousness (ṣidqātĕkā)" — the divine righteousness that establishes what is right and acts to bring it about in the covenant community.
8: "The LORD is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and rich in steadfast love" — ḥannûn wĕraḥûm YHWH ʾerek ʾappayim ûgĕdal-ḥāsed. The fourth appearance of the Sinai self-revelation formula (Exodus 34:6) in Psalms 86–145 (also appearing in Psalms 86:15; Psalms 103:8; Psalms 111:4; Psalms 112:4; Psalms 116:5; Psalms 145:8). Psalm 145's acrostic structure places this formula at the ḥeth-verse (Psalms 145:8), suggesting the psalmist deliberately positioned the central divine character confession within the alphabetic praise. In rabbinic tradition, this verse became one of the "Thirteen Attributes" (Middot) of God recited in liturgy, especially on Yom Kippur.
9: "The LORD is good to all; his compassion extends over all he has made" — ṭôb YHWH lakkōl wĕraḥămāyw ʿal-kol-maʿăśāyw. The universal scope of divine goodness and compassion — "to all (lakkōl)" and "over all he has made (ʿal-kol-maʿăśāyw)" — is a striking extension of covenant character to all creatures, not merely to Israel. The Psalter's universalism reaches its clearest expression here: YHWH's raḥămîm (compassion — from reḥem, womb) extends over the full creation. This verse was cited by early church fathers as evidence of divine common grace and is foundational for Jewish and Christian theologies of creation-care.
10: "All your works will praise you, LORD, and your devoted ones will bless you" — yôdûkā YHWH kol-maʿăśêkā wăḥăsîdêkā yĕbārĕkûkā. Two rings of praise: all creation (all your works) in the outer ring, and the ḥăsîdîm (devoted ones, those characterized by covenant loyalty) in the inner ring. The ḥăsîdîm are the community of those who reflect YHWH's own ḥesed back to him in worship and toward neighbor in action. Their blessing is not merely the passive praise of creation but the active, intentional declaration of the covenant community who know what they are praising.
11: "They will speak of the glory of your kingdom and tell of your power" — the middle section of Psalm 145 (Psalms 145:11–13) is the theological heart of the psalm: the divine kingdom (malkût). "Glory of your kingdom (kĕbôd malkûtekā)" — malkût (kingdom, kingship, rule) becomes the central term: YHWH as king is not merely a metaphor but the foundational political claim of Israel's theology. All earthly kings are derivative of and accountable to this kingship. The NT's proclamation "the kingdom of God has come" (Mark 1:15) is unintelligible without this OT substrate.
12: "to make known to all humanity your mighty deeds and the majestic glory of your kingdom" — the purpose clause: the witness of the ḥăsîdîm is not merely internal community praise but external proclamation "to all humanity (lĕbĕnê hāʾādām, to the children of adam)." The theological vision of Ps 145 is therefore both doxological (praise to YHWH) and missional (proclamation to the nations). The "majestic glory of his kingdom (ûkĕbôd hădār malkûtô)" echoes the triple-glory language of Psalms 145:5 — the kingdom possesses the same superlative glory as the King.
13: "Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and your dominion endures through all generations" — malkûtĕkā malkût kol-ʿōlāmîm ûmemšaltĕkā bĕkol-dôr wādôr (your kingdom is a kingdom of all ages; your dominion in every generation and generation). The Dead Sea Scrolls version (11QPsᵃ) adds at this point a nun-verse that is absent from the MT: "God is faithful in his words and holy in all his works" — reflecting either a scribal omission in the MT tradition or an alternate text. The LXX also has this verse. The kingdom's eternity is the counterpoint to human transience (Psalms 145:4: "his days pass like a shadow") — the kingdom lasts precisely what the human leader does not.
14: "The LORD upholds all who are falling and lifts up all who are brought low" — sômēk YHWH lĕkol-hahannōfĕlîm wĕzôqēp lĕkol-hakkĕpûpîm. The two participles — "those who are falling (nōfĕlîm)" and "those who are brought low (kĕpûpîm)" — describe those on the way down. YHWH "upholds (sômēk)" and "lifts up (zôqēp)" them — the two verbs of divine intervention at the point of collapse. This verse became liturgically significant: the Jewish Amidah prayer contains the blessing Mĕḥayyē haMētîm (who revives the dead), which draws on the logic of this verse — the God who lifts those who have fallen can also raise the dead.
15: "The eyes of all look to you, and you give them their food in due season" — ʿênê-kōl ʾēlêkā yĕśabbērû wĕʾattāh nôtēn-lāhem et-ʾoklām bĕʿittô. The image of all creatures looking expectantly toward YHWH for their food is a creation-theology statement about universal dependence. "In due season (bĕʿittô)" — the seasonal gift of food; YHWH provides within the created order of seasons and harvests, not through continuous miracle but through faithful creation-management. This verse and the next (Psalms 145:16) are used as grace before meals in Jewish practice, chosen because they express the theological meaning of eating: receiving from the open hand of the creator.
16: "You open your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing" — pôtēaḥ et-yādekā ûmaśbîaʿ lĕkol-ḥāy rāṣôn (you open your hand and satisfy every living thing's desire). "Open your hand" — the divine generosity expressed as an open, giving hand (the inverse of the closed or grabbing hand of the oppressor). Rāṣôn (desire, goodwill, favor) — each creature's appropriate desire is satisfied; the word carries overtones of covenantal goodwill. The created order is not a system of competition for scarce resources but a field of divine generosity in which YHWH's open hand provides for all. Psalms 104:27–28 uses the same image: "All creatures look to you to give them their food at the proper time. When you give it to them, they gather it up."
17: "The LORD is righteous in all his ways and faithful in all his works" — ṣaddîq YHWH bĕkol-dĕrākāyw wĕḥāsîd bĕkol-maʿăśāyw. This verse is close to the missing nun-verse of Psalms 145:13a in the Dead Sea Scrolls version; the similarities suggest textual relationship. "Righteous in all his ways (bĕkol-dĕrākāyw)" — the totality-formula ("in all") appears five times in Psalms 145:13–20, creating a cumulative universalism. Ḥāsîd applied to YHWH himself — YHWH as the one who practices ḥesed. The ḥăsîdîm (Psalms 145:10) model themselves on a God who is himself ḥāsîd.
18: "The LORD is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in sincerity" — qārôb YHWH lĕkol-qōrĕʾāyw lĕkol ʾăšer yiqrāʾûhû beʾĕmet. "Near to all who call (qārôb lĕkol-qōrĕʾāyw)" — divine nearness as the answer to prayer; the God who is enthroned in the heavens (Psalms 113:5; Psalms 123:1) is simultaneously near to the one who calls (Deuteronomy 4:7: "What other nation is so great as to have their gods near them the way the LORD our God is near us whenever we pray to him?"). "In sincerity (beʾĕmet)" — in truth, faithfulness, with integrity of intention; prayer from the lips only without the heart is not true calling on YHWH (Isaiah 29:13; Matthew 15:8).
19: "He fulfills the desire of those who fear him; he hears their cry and saves them" — yirʾāt ʾōtô (the fear/reverence of him). The "fear of the LORD (yirʾat YHWH)" is the wisdom tradition's foundational disposition — the right orientation toward the Creator that produces moral formation, practical wisdom, and covenant loyalty (Proverbs 1:7; Psalms 9:10; Psalms 14:27; Psalms 111:10; Ecclesiastes 12:13). Those who fear YHWH are the same as the ḥăsîdîm of Psalms 145:10 — the community shaped by covenant reverence whose desires YHWH fulfills. The desires of the fearers are not arbitrary wishes but the covenant-formed longings of those whose desires have been shaped by relationship with YHWH.
20: "The LORD guards all who love him, but he will destroy all the wicked" — the binary division of humanity in the Psalter's moral vision: those who love YHWH (ʾōhăbāyw) are guarded; the wicked (rĕšāʿîm) are destroyed. "Guards (šômēr)" — the covenant protection promised to those within covenant relationship (Psalms 121:7–8; Psalms 127:1; Psalms 145:20). "Destroy (yašmîd)" — the covenant judgment on those outside covenant relationship. The sharp binary is not cruel but structurally necessary: a moral universe where YHWH reigns must ultimately distinguish between covenant loyalty and covenant-breaking.
21: "My mouth will speak the praise of the LORD, and let all living things bless his holy name forever and ever" — the psalm ends as it began: with the speaker's personal praise (Psalms 145:1: "I will exalt you") and then universalizes it to "all living things (kol-bāśār, all flesh)." The acrostic that covered every letter of the Hebrew alphabet is now extended to every creature — the alphabetic completion of praise points toward the cosmic completion. "Forever and ever (lĕʿôlām waʿed)" — the temporal frame that matches the eternal scope of the kingdom (Psalms 145:13). The psalm whose Hebrew name names the whole book ends with a vision of all flesh praising YHWH's holy name eternally.
